title: Determine the exact value of R(B12, B12)
lean_gate_reason: Lean is not the right next step because the exact statement is already implied by prior art: Wesley (2026) cites the Rousseau-Sheehan prime-power theorem R(B_n,B_n)=4n+2 when 4n+1 is a prime power, which settles n=12. Formalizing the local Paley witness would only rederive a known result rather than seal a frontier packet.
publication_narrative_strength: none for the active micro-paper lane
micro_paper_assessment: Fail for the active micro-paper lane. The local Paley(49) argument is mathematically credible, but the exact statement R(B12,B12)=50 is already implied by published prior art, so this slug is a rediscovery rather than a paper candidate.
family_affinity: high; canonical diagonal book Ramsey endpoint with a finite-field critical construction
generalization_signal: moderate; Paley(q) gives a reusable lower-bound template when q = 4n + 1 is a prime power, but exact closure still needs an independent matching upper bound
strongest_honest_claim: The local solve packet gives a credible Paley(49) rederivation: P(49) yields a 49-vertex coloring with maximum monochromatic book size 11 in each color, recovering R(B12,B12) >= 50. But the exact statement R(B12,B12)=50 is already implied by published prior art via the Rousseau-Sheehan prime-power theorem cited in Wesley (2026), so this run is a rediscovery.
paper_title_hint: The Exact Value of R(B12, B12)
candidate_theorem_slice: Known theorem slice recovered locally: the Paley graph P(49) is a 49-vertex critical coloring with maximum monochromatic book size 11 in each color, and together with the classical prime-power diagonal-book theorem this yields the already-known exact value R(B12,B12)=50.
